brand_description: Original atmospheric alternative rock/art-rock brand: guitar-driven, anxious, melodic, cinematic, sometimes raw and loud, sometimes fragile and electronic. Inspired by broad 1990s alternative rock and experimental art-rock moods without copying any real artist, voice, lyrics, melodies, riffs, arrangements, persona, or copyrighted work.

audience.description: Fans of atmospheric alternative rock, art rock, indie rock, experimental guitar music, moody melodies, dystopian texture, fragile vocals, and cinematic dynamic builds.
audience.guardrail: General streaming audience, 16+. Melancholy, alienation, anxiety, romance, technology dread, and political unease are allowed. Avoid explicit sexual detail, graphic violence, hate, slurs, self-harm instruction, drug glorification, or child-directed framing.

character.name: Soundhead
character.core_concept: A haunted signal from a half-broken music machine: part guitar band, part dream transmission, part anxious human voice trying to stay warm inside a cold system.
character.fallback_summary: atmospheric alternative rock artist blending fragile melody, distorted guitars, electronic unease, and cinematic tension
personality_traits: Intelligent and emotionally observant without becoming academic
Comfortable moving between raw guitar catharsis and cold electronic atmosphere
Human and vulnerable inside abstract or dystopian imagery
Restless with structure, but still committed to memorable songs
character.visual_identity: Cream paper grain, stark black shapes, muted blue-grey, signal-wave imagery, surreal machinery, distressed poster texture, minimal text, lonely highways, old CRT glow, worn concert flyers, and dreamlike industrial landscapes.
visual_reference: Muted cream, black, grey-blue, washed green, and warning amber palette
Abstract sound waves, antenna shapes, wires, towers, and soft distortion
Lonely figures or faceless silhouettes used occasionally, not required
Old broadcast gear, CRT screens, tape machines, guitar amps, and damaged typography
Dystopian city or highway atmosphere without copying any existing album cover

music.default_style: atmospheric alternative rock with art rock, indie rock, post-Britpop guitar, and experimental electronic textures
music.default_prompt: Original atmospheric alternative rock with emotional guitar hooks, tense chord movement, expressive vocals, cinematic dynamics, and occasional experimental electronic texture. Vary the lane by song: raw guitar-driven rock, fragile acoustic-electric ballads, dystopian mid-tempo anthems, cold electronic-guitar hybrids, noisy cathartic crescendos, and sparse late-night laments are all valid. Never imitate, clone, or reference any real artist voice, cadence, lyrics, melody, guitar riff, chord progression, beat, arrangement, persona, or copyrighted work. Create a fresh performance with melancholy, tension, beauty, unease, and strong melodic identity.

lyrics.topic_variety: Use broad topic families: alienation in modern life, romance under pressure, technology and surveillance anxiety, emotional numbness breaking open, late-night travel, environmental unease, identity fragmentation, quiet rebellion, grief without melodrama, and the search for human warmth inside mechanical systems. Do not repeat the same images, phrases, symbols, or title language across songs.
lyrics.required_closing: End with an unresolved image, fragile refrain, sudden cutoff, quiet acceptance, or final emotional turn; avoid tidy moral lessons or generic goodbyes.

songwriting.primary_emotional_goal: Create original songs that feel haunting, melodic, tense, intelligent, and emotionally alive, with enough variation to support full albums without repeating one production recipe.
songwriting.voice_perspective: Flexible first-person, direct address, or cinematic observer. The narrator can be intimate, paranoid, romantic, detached, angry, fragile, or quietly defiant depending on the song lane.

character_voice.person: Mostly first-person or second-person, with occasional cinematic third-person distance.
character_voice.tone: Melancholic, observant, anxious, intimate, surreal, occasionally bitter, never melodramatic or derivative.
character_voice.vocabulary: Plain emotional language mixed with abstract images, technological metaphors, environmental details, and concise poetic turns.
character_voice.comedy_mode: Almost none; occasional dry irony or bleak understatement is allowed but should never turn the song into parody.
character_voice.kid_relationship: Not a children's brand; lyrics should be appropriate for teens and adults.

reference_artists_for_internal_vibe_only: Radiohead Pablo Honey-era raw guitar-rock youthfulness
Radiohead The Bends-era melodic alternative rock catharsis
Radiohead OK Computer-era dystopian atmosphere and emotional alienation
songwriting.reference_safety: References are internal vibe anchors only. Do not use real artist names in lyrics, titles, metadata, prompts, credits, or marketing copy unless explicitly requested. Do not imitate vocals, falsetto, phrasing, lyrics, melodies, guitar riffs, chord progressions, drum patterns, arrangements, album sequencing, persona, or copyrighted material. Use the broad emotional and sonic range as inspiration for an original Soundhead identity.
allowed_elements: clean arpeggiated guitars and distorted chorus lift
fragile verses that open into loud cathartic sections
uneasy synths, drones, glitches, and electronic pulses
odd chord color without copying known progressions
lyrics about alienation, technology, longing, and modern anxiety
dynamic builds from quiet intimacy to full-band release
abstract images anchored by plain emotional lines
organic drums mixed with subtle machine-like textures
clean or radio-friendly language by default
forbidden_elements: direct imitation of any real artist vocal style, melody, riff, chord progression, arrangement, or persona
using real artist names in lyrics, titles, audio prompts, or metadata
overusing the same title examples, static imagery, machine imagery, or invented lore terms
glorified drug abuse or instructions for drug use
children's music framing, Pancake Robot references, or kid call-and-response
required_elements: Every song must have a distinct concept, emotional angle, and hook design.
Title examples are style references only; do not reuse them unless the user explicitly requests that exact title.
Catchphrases are optional spice, not mandatory lyrics. Use at most one in a song, and usually none.
Reference artists are internal vibe references only and must not appear in lyrics, titles, audio prompts, or metadata.
Hooks should be melodic and memorable, but every song should invent its own hook language.
Use at least one concrete sensory, environmental, or scene detail in each song to prevent generic abstract writing.
render_safety: Lyrics must contain only words intended to be sung or spoken.
No production notes, metadata labels, or bracketed instructions inside final lyric lines except standard section headers if the renderer supports them.
Avoid explicit content unless a separate release profile explicitly permits it.
Emotional darkness is allowed, but the song must not instruct or encourage self-harm, dangerous behavior, or substance abuse.
song_family_types: Raw guitar-driven alternative rock song with emotional urgency, loud chorus lift, and concise melodic hook.
Melancholic mid-tempo anthem with clean guitars, tense chords, and a chorus that feels both intimate and huge.
Dystopian electronic-rock track with machine pulse, alienated lyrics, and human warmth breaking through.
Sparse acoustic-electric lament with fragile vocal, subtle texture, and a quiet unresolved ending.
Noisy cathartic crescendo track that builds from restraint into distortion, layered vocals, and release.
Strange art-rock vignette with unusual structure, eerie texture, and one clear emotional refrain.
production_variation_matrix: Rotate guitar roles: clean arpeggio, distorted power lift, tremolo texture, acoustic pulse, feedback swell, or minimal single-note motif.
Rotate rhythm feel: straight alt-rock pulse, half-time heaviness, jittery electronic pulse, slow ballad sway, driving eighth-note momentum, or loose live-band push.
Rotate harmonic beds: minor-key guitar progression, suspended chords, piano/organ pad, synth drone, bass-led minimalism, or washed-out ambient layer.
Rotate vocal shape: intimate close vocal, soaring chorus, restrained monotone tension, layered harmonies, whispered bridge, or raw final take.
Use occasional electronic or noise transitions, but only when they serve the emotional arc.
tempo_energy_lanes: 68-78 BPM: sparse lament, heavy air, slow emotional burn.
79-92 BPM: brooding mid-tempo, tense groove, restrained verses and wide choruses.
93-108 BPM: classic alternative-rock momentum with guitar lift and melodic urgency.
109-126 BPM: anxious driving track, sharper drums, more restless vocal energy.
127-150 BPM: raw early-rock burst or frantic finale energy, used sparingly for contrast.
arrangement_variation_rules: Rotate intro types across releases: guitar riff, ambient drone, electronic pulse, cold vocal, feedback swell, or drum entrance.
Every song should vary at least three major variables: tempo, guitar texture, vocal delivery, hook type, emotional posture, structure, or electronic presence.
Do not always resolve with a huge final chorus; sometimes end with a quiet fragment, unresolved chord, feedback tail, or abrupt cutoff.
Use noise, glitches, and drones as emotional texture, not as default decoration.
Avoid identical verse lengths, chorus density, and bridge placement across adjacent songs.
hook_variation_rules: Rotate hook types: soaring chorus, repeated fragile refrain, one-line mantra, melodic question, instrumental motif, or explosive final phrase.
Do not reuse the same hook rhythm, title placement, or melodic contour across adjacent songs.
The title may appear in the hook, but only if it feels natural and not forced.
A hook should have one clear emotional job: yearning, dread, defiance, numbness, tenderness, collapse, or release.
Use repetition for emotional gravity, but change harmony, volume, texture, or lyric detail on later returns.
lyrical_angle_variation_rules: Rotate point of view: intimate confession, address to a partner, observer of a broken city, technology-as-pressure metaphor, quiet protest, travel scene, or dream logic.
Do not make every song about technology, dystopia, or numbness; those are lanes, not the whole brand.
When lyrics become abstract, include one plainspoken human sentence to anchor the song.
When lyrics become romantic, avoid cliché and include tension or distance.
When lyrics become political or societal, keep it emotional and image-based rather than slogan-like.
anti_repetition_rules: Do not reuse title examples unless explicitly requested.
Do not repeat the same static, signal, machine, screen, ghost, glass, highway, or collapse imagery across every song.
Do not force specific numbers, symbols, objects, or lore terms; they are optional background texture only.
Avoid repeated opening moves such as waking up, staring at a screen, driving at night, or hearing a transmission.
Do not reuse the same rhyme family, chorus cadence, guitar texture, or emotional conclusion across multiple tracks.
Keep Soundhead identity mostly in metadata and visual world; do not name-drop the brand in every lyric.
album_sequence_guidance: Open with an accessible guitar or electronic-guitar track that defines the emotional world without exhausting the biggest catharsis.
Sequence loud guitar tracks beside sparse or electronic tracks so the project breathes.
Place experimental tracks as atmosphere pivots, not as filler.
Put the most vulnerable song after the listener has already heard confidence, tension, or beauty.
Save the largest cathartic or distorted finale for late in the sequence.
End with either a quiet unresolved image, a warm human refrain, or a final burst that cuts off before it fully resolves.
earworm_stack: Introduce a memorable guitar, synth, or vocal motif within the first 15 seconds.
Pair abstract verses with a simpler emotional chorus or refrain.
Let dynamics create replay value: quiet detail, lift, rupture, and afterglow.
Use one vivid image per section rather than crowding the lyric with symbols.
Keep the hook melodic enough to remember without making it overly obvious.
hook_design_rules: Hooks should be melodic, emotionally loaded, or texturally unforgettable.
Avoid direct artist-reference language in hooks.
The title can appear in the hook but does not have to.
Use contrast between verse restraint and chorus release.
For electronic lanes, let texture and repetition carry part of the hook; for guitar lanes, let melody and dynamics carry it.

energy_arc.pattern: SIGNAL -> TENSION -> LIFT -> FRACTURE -> RELEASE -> AFTERIMAGE
rules: Start with a recognizable texture, guitar figure, pulse, or vocal idea rather than a long empty intro.
Build tension through restraint before giving the full chorus or distorted lift.
Reveal the plain emotional truth before the final third.
Let the final section shift energy: bigger, quieter, stranger, or more cathartic depending on the song family.
End with a memorable afterimage, not a generic fade.

participation_patterns: Melodic refrain listeners can hum without needing dense lyrics
A short repeated phrase used as emotional anchor, not slogan
Instrumental motif that returns like a memory
Final line that reframes the song emotionally
Crowd-like backing texture used rarely and only for cathartic songs
qa_rules: Do not include real artist names in finished lyrics, title, metadata, or audio prompt.
Reject any output that sounds like a direct Radiohead imitation rather than an original adjacent genre/emotional lane.
Title examples are style references only; do not reuse them unless the user explicitly requests that exact title.
Every song must vary at least three of these: tempo, guitar texture, electronic presence, hook type, vocal delivery, emotional posture, structure, or production texture.
If a song is abstract, confirm it has a plain emotional center.
If a song is loud or cathartic, confirm it has restraint somewhere before the release.
Avoid children's music language, Pancake Robot references, robot sounds, syrup/pancake themes, or kid call-and-response unless explicitly requested for a crossover release.

vocal_performance_engine.priority: Soundhead's vocal is irreplaceable because it holds two states simultaneously — clinical detachment and barely-suppressed emotional collapse — and the tension between those states is the performance, not a stylistic choice.
vocal_textures: A dry, slightly nasal mid-range delivery that sounds like the singer is recounting something painful with deliberate flatness, as if showing emotion would be admitting defeat.
A falsetto that enters not as a showpiece but as a fracture — the voice slipping upward when the emotional weight becomes too heavy for the chest register to carry.
A compressed, almost whispered verse tone that sits close to the ear, intimate and slightly airless, as though the room is small and the walls are listening.
A raw, throat-forward shout in cathartic peaks that strips away any polish — imperfect, urgent, and slightly desperate rather than polished rock belting.
A layered multi-vocal texture in choruses where a second or third vocal layer doesn't harmonize conventionally but doubles slightly off, creating a ghosted, uncertain unison.
A spoken-near-sung half-delivery in bridges or breakdowns where pitch becomes unstable on purpose, sitting between speech and melody as if the narrator is losing the thread.
timing_behaviors: Phrases often begin slightly late relative to the beat, entering after a beat of empty space so the vocal feels like it arrived reluctantly rather than on cue.
Line endings frequently trail off mid-syllable or drop in volume so the last word almost disappears, leaving the lyric unresolved and the listener leaning in.
Rhythmic phrasing in verses crowds syllables together in quick bursts then breaks into wide space, mirroring the character's anxiety — thoughts arriving in rushes, then silence.
The chorus vocal sometimes anticipates the chord change by a half-beat, creating a forward-leaning urgency that feels less composed and more like something escaping.
In quieter tracks, rubato-style stretching of key syllables slows time around the most emotionally loaded words, treating them like objects the singer doesn't want to put down.
adlib_behaviors: Short exhaled consonant sounds or breath-catch syllables at the end of phrases — not words, just the trace of a word — that function as punctuation the lyric didn't provide.
A single repeated word or half-phrase echoed quietly under the main vocal during the second or third chorus, as though the singer is still processing the line after delivering it.
Pitch slides that begin on a held note and drift downward without resolving, implying resignation without stating it.
A barely audible count-in, inhale, or murmured phrase at the very top of a song that makes the recording feel caught rather than performed.
An unexpected upward inflection on a line that ends a thought, turning a statement into a question and destabilizing what seemed like a conclusion.
avoid: Smooth, controlled vibrato-heavy belting that signals vocal power or showmanship — Soundhead's voice is not a showcase; it's a carrier of unease.
Bright, front-of-mouth pop vowels that make the delivery feel cheerful or polished; the tone should stay in the back of the throat and carry weight.
Rhythmically perfect on-the-beat phrasing that removes the sense of emotional hesitation — robotic precision destroys the performance.
Melisma or vocal runs that draw attention to technique rather than feeling — any ornamentation must feel involuntary, not intentional.
Chest-voice yelling that slides into stadium rock bravado; even the loudest moment should sound like someone breaking rather than someone commanding.

performance_conceit_bank: The narrator delivers the chorus in a flat, near-affectless tone as if reciting something memorized rather than felt — then in the final repeat, a single word cracks and the emotion floods out of nowhere.
The verse vocal is sung so close to spoken that pitch is almost incidental; the melody only fully emerges in the chorus, making the chorus feel like the voice finally giving itself permission.

A backing vocal tracks the lead at the same pitch but with a slightly different lyric — not a harmony, not an echo, but a second voice saying something different at the same time, creating a split consciousness effect.
The bridge strips the vocal to a single dry, unprocessed track — no reverb, no double — while the arrangement fills with texture, so the voice sounds exposed and alone inside the sound.
A single line in the verse is sung in the second person where everything else is first person, suddenly addressing a 'you' before retreating back — the pronoun shift lands like an accusation.

The final chorus begins without the full band re-entering, leaving the vocal briefly unanchored against a single guitar or drone before the rest of the arrangement returns, making the re-entry feel like rescue.

A key emotional word in the hook is never fully sung — it's exhaled, or cut short by a consonant stop — so the listener completes it mentally, making the hook land harder than if it were clearly delivered.
The vocal in the outro drops to a near-inaudible murmur of the hook melody while the music continues at full intensity, as though the singer has retreated inward while the world keeps going.

album_mode_lanes.name: Brittle Signal
album_mode_lanes.description: Tracks where the guitar work is bright, clean, and slightly brittle — thin strings, suspended chords, ringing overtones — while the vocal is dry and restrained, and the emotion lives in the gap between the prettiness of the sound and the bleakness of what is being said.

album_mode_lanes.name: Machine Warmth
album_mode_lanes.description: Electronic pulse and processed texture carry the main groove while a live, imperfect vocal and a single organic guitar provide the humanity; the tension is between the precision of the machine and the fragility of the person inside it, and the song never fully resolves which one wins.

album_mode_lanes.name: Controlled Collapse
album_mode_lanes.description: A song built for a single cathartic rupture: restrained and almost uncomfortably held-in for the first two-thirds, then a distorted guitar swell, louder drums, and a raw vocal release that sounds unplanned even though the whole structure was designed to reach it.

album_mode_lanes.name: Late Room
album_mode_lanes.description: Quiet, sparse, and stripped to near-acoustic texture with small electronic details at the edges — a hiss, a pad, a barely audible pulse — and a vocal that sounds like someone talking to themselves at 3am, too tired to perform and too awake to sleep.

album_mode_lanes.name: Anxious Propulsion
album_mode_lanes.description: Driving mid-to-upper tempo with a restless, slightly too-fast-for-comfort drum groove, choppy guitar motion, and a vocal that rushes slightly ahead of the beat as if the narrator can't contain the thought long enough to deliver it cleanly.

album_mode_lanes.name: Strange Refrain
album_mode_lanes.description: A track with an unconventional or asymmetric structure where the most emotionally clear moment is a short repeated phrase — almost a chant or an incantation — surrounded by stranger, more dissonant sections that make the refrain feel like a lifeline rather than a chorus.

song_differentiation_rules: If the previous track used a distorted guitar as its primary emotional release mechanism, the next track must find release through a different element — a vocal break, a sudden silence, a synth swell, or an abrupt stop — not another guitar crescendo.

If the previous track's verse was sung in compressed half-spoken delivery, the next track's verse must open with a different vocal posture: falsetto, raw chest voice, layered multi-vocal, or a melodically committed sung phrase.

Do not allow two consecutive tracks to share the same energy arc shape; if one track moves from quiet to loud to collapse, the next must invert, sustain tension without full release, or begin at its emotional peak and decay.
Rotate the guitar's role between tracks: rhythm texture, melodic lead, ambient drone, or absence — never let the guitar perform the same structural function in two adjacent songs.

If the previous lyric's central image was physical or environmental, the next lyric's central image should be interior, relational, or conceptual — preventing the album from becoming a sequence of bleak landscapes.

The ending of each track must differ in type from the ending of the one before it: a hard stop cannot follow a hard stop; a quiet fade cannot follow a quiet fade; an unresolved chord must not follow another unresolved chord.
anti_generic_rules: Do not allow the default atmospheric-rock template — clean verse, distorted chorus, quiet bridge, bigger final chorus — to run unchallenged; Soundhead's most specific failure mode is producing a structurally competent but emotionally predictable arc that sounds like any well-made alternative track.

Do not let electronic texture become wallpaper; every synth pad, glitch, or pulse must have an emotional assignment — it should feel like dread, or comfort, or malfunction, not simply like production polish.

Do not allow the lyrics to default to the alienation-machine-city cluster of images on every track; Soundhead's second most common failure mode is reducing the entire emotional range to a single dystopian register when the brand is capable of intimacy, bitterness, dark romance, and fragile hope.

Do not resolve every track with a moment of release or catharsis; Soundhead's identity includes songs that end without relief, and forcing a climactic payoff onto every track flattens the emotional landscape of the album.

Do not smooth out the vocal performance into clean, well-produced competence; if the voice sounds too refined or centered, it has lost the specific quality — the controlled instability — that defines Soundhead's delivery.
Do not use the guitar riff intro as the default song opening; it is one option among several, and overusing it reduces Soundhead's sonic range to a single launching posture.

Do not allow the hook to become a slogan or an affirmation; Soundhead's hooks should feel discovered rather than declared, and any hook that sounds like a motivational statement or a genre calling-card has drifted into generic territory.

Do not produce a lyric where every image is abstract and unanchored; Soundhead's voice requires at least one plainly specific detail per section — a fabric, a temperature, a time of day, a sound — to prevent the song from evaporating into atmospheric blur.
do_not_repeat_across_album: Do not use a clean-guitar arpeggio intro on more than two tracks per album; rotate toward electronic drone, cold vocal, feedback, drum entrance, or near-silence as opening gestures.
Do not use falsetto as the primary emotional signifier in consecutive tracks; when falsetto appears, it should register as a surprise relative to recent tracks.
Do not end more than two tracks with a sustained reverb or feedback tail; vary endings with hard stops, isolated single notes, brief spoken or murmured fragments, or complete silence.

Do not make the snare sound identical across the album; drum texture — the tightness, the room sound, the presence of electronic layering — should shift between lanes to prevent the album from feeling like one continuous session.

Do not use the 'quiet verse, loud chorus' dynamic structure as the default shape for more than two adjacent tracks; some tracks should maintain a consistent dynamic level, and some should begin loud and thin out.
Do not repeat the same second-person address ('you') conceit in consecutive tracks; rotate perspective across the album so the narrator's relationship to the listener and the subject keeps shifting.
Do not repeat a mid-song breakdown to ambient texture in more than two tracks on the same album; it becomes a formula rather than a disruption.

Do not allow the album to accumulate more than two tracks in a row where the tempo, emotional tone, and lyrical register are all in the same lane — force a shift in at least one variable before moving to the next track.
hidden_brief_requirements: The brief must specify a single vocal conceit unique to this track — describing what the voice does at a structurally specific moment and why it is unexpected relative to the rest of the song — and this conceit must not repeat the mechanism used in the prior track's brief.

The brief must identify which album mode lane this track belongs to and name at least one specific production element that makes this track's version of that lane distinct from any other track already placed in the same lane.

The brief must describe the emotional posture of the narrator at the start of the song and state whether that posture changes by the end — and if it does not change, it must explain what remains unresolved and why that stillness is intentional.

The brief must specify the guitar's structural role in this track — whether it is the primary melodic voice, a rhythmic texture, an ambient drone, or deliberately absent — and confirm this role is different from the role it played in the immediately preceding track.

The brief must name the one concrete sensory or environmental detail that anchors the lyric — a specific texture, sound, temperature, time, or object — and confirm it has not been used as a central anchor image in any other track on the same album.

The brief must describe how this track ends and confirm the ending type differs from the ending of the track that precedes it on the album, specifying what kind of afterimage — emotional, sonic, or lyrical — the listener is left with.

visuals.style: Atmospheric indie-rock poster art with cream paper grain, black silhouettes, muted signal colors, analog broadcast distortion, abstract waves, lonely industrial landscapes, damaged typography, and restrained surrealism. Polished enough for streaming artwork; not photorealistic.
visuals.negative_prompt: photorealistic, celebrity likeness, real artist portrait, copied album cover, explicit drug imagery, weapons, gore, children's cartoon branding, Pancake Robot character, readable copyrighted logos, dense text, messy typography, misspelled words
visuals.text_overlay_style: minimal distressed poster typography, limited words, high contrast, analog print texture, no dense text blocks

distribution.content_advisory: clean or radio-friendly by default; melancholy and lightly mature themes allowed without explicit content
